result:
There is a certain quality of light in the late afternoon, especially in the transitional seasons of autumn and spring, that feels like a memory before it has even passed. It’s a syrupy, golden light that slants through windows at an impossible angle, illuminating dust motes in the air and turning them into a silent, swirling galaxy. This is the light of quietude, the light of unspoken things. It’s in this light that I often find myself thinking about the background hum of existence.

We live our lives largely in the foreground. Our attention is a spotlight, flitting from the urgent email to the rumbling stomach, from the conversation in front of us to the to-do list spooling out in our minds. We are creatures of the explicit, the loud, the demanding. We navigate the peaks and troughs of our days, focusing on the tasks, the interactions, the goals. But beneath all of that, there is a constant, subtle hum.

In childhood, this hum was the ambient sound of safety. It was the distant drone of a lawnmower on a Saturday, the rhythmic clinking of dishes being put away in the kitchen, the muffled dialogue from a television in another room. It was the sound of a world that was functioning without your direct input. You could be lying on the floor, lost in a book or the patterns of the carpet, and this gentle symphony of domesticity was the score to your small, contained universe. It was a sound you didn't even notice until it was gone—the sudden, piercing silence when the power went out, or the unsettling quiet of an empty house. That’s when you realized the hum had been holding you, a sonic blanket you never knew you needed.

As we get older, the hum changes. It becomes less external and more internal. The gentle sounds of a functioning world are replaced by the persistent, low-grade thrum of anxiety. It’s the background process running in your mind: the worry about rent, the mental replay of an awkward conversation, the weight of unspoken expectations, the awareness of time slipping away like sand through a clenched fist. This new hum is not comforting; it’s draining. It’s the engine of obligation that never quite shuts off, even in sleep.

We have, in response, become masters of drowning it out. We fill every pocket of silence with a podcast, a playlist, a video. The commute is not for staring out the window; it’s for catching up on content. The wait in line is not for observing the people around you; it’s for a quick scroll through the curated lives of others. We have traded the quiet, external hum of childhood for a loud, internal one, and then we have sought to deafen ourselves to it with an even louder, more insistent digital static.

We mistake this constant stimulation for engagement, for living a full life. But I suspect it is the opposite. It is a flight from life, a flight from the self. To be truly present with oneself is to be willing to sit in that slanted, golden light and listen to the internal hum without reaching for a distraction. It is to acknowledge the anxieties, the fears, the quiet sadnesses that make up its frequency, and to let them be. It is to notice, without judgment, the cacophony within.

Sometimes, I manage to find the old hum again. It happens unexpectedly. It might be during a walk without headphones, when the only sounds are the rustle of leaves and the scuff of my own shoes on the pavement. My internal monologue, that frantic narrator of my life, finally runs out of things to say. The anxious hum recedes, and for a moment, I can hear the world again. The chirp of a sparrow, the distant wail of a siren, the wind moving through the branches of a pine tree. It’s the same principle as childhood—the reassuring sound of a world functioning without my direct input. It’s a profound relief, a reminder of my own smallness in the grand, beautiful indifference of it all.

Or it might happen while doing something utterly mundane, like washing the dishes. The warmth of the water on my hands, the repetitive motion, the simple, tangible goal of clean plates. The mind, bored with the lack of complex stimuli, gives up its frantic churning. In that space, a different kind of thought can emerge—not a worry or a plan, but a genuine curiosity, a forgotten memory, a sudden, quiet understanding of a problem that had seemed intractable. This is the space where creativity and peace reside. It is the space we are so desperate to avoid.

We have been conditioned to believe that stillness is a waste of time. Productivity is our god, and efficiency is its prophet. But the soul, or the psyche, or whatever you wish to call that innermost part of us, does not operate on a schedule. It cannot be optimized with an app. It requires fallow periods. It needs the unproductive, aimless, quiet moments to sift through the debris of our lives, to integrate our experiences, to heal. It needs to listen to the hum.

That late-afternoon light is fading now as I write this. The gold is deepening into a bruised orange and purple. The day is preparing to cede to the night. Soon, the artificial lights will come on, and the explicit demands of the evening will take over. But for a few more minutes, there is this pause. This liminal space. The hum of the refrigerator has become audible. A car passes on the street outside, its sound growing and then fading, a temporary story in the larger silence. There is a peace in this moment that feels ancient and deeply necessary. It’s the feeling of being a small part of a vast, breathing world, a world that will continue its gentle, indifferent humming long after my own internal noise has ceased for good. And in that thought, there is not fear, but a strange and quiet comfort.
